title: Readable text metrics

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Readable text metrics; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Readable text metrics
sources.claim: Readable typography balances line length, line height, spacing, alignment, hierarchy, and user zoom without fixing text into inflexible boxes.

sources.locator: MDN CSS Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Readable text metrics
sources.claim: Readable typography balances line length, line height, spacing, alignment, hierarchy, and user zoom without fixing text into inflexible boxes.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Readable typography balances line length, line height, spacing, alignment, hierarchy, and user zoom without fixing text into inflexible boxes.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Article text uses a bounded measure and unitless line-height while containers expand for longer translated labels. Your goal is to create and audit readable body, heading and code typography at 200 percent zoom. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Lengths, percentages and viewport units; html semantics text; Font families and loading; html semantics headings. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Readable typography balances line length, line height, spacing, alignment, hierarchy, and user zoom without fixing text into inflexible boxes. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Optimal values vary by script, language, content, and font; user overrides and 200 percent zoom must remain usable. This draft therefore rejects: Smaller text always fits more content and therefore improves usability.

includes: Set scalable size, line height, measure, weight, alignment and spacing without blocking text zoom.
Create and audit readable body, heading and code typography at 200 percent zoom.
excludes: Optimal values vary by script, language, content, and font; user overrides and 200 percent zoom must remain usable.

outcomes: Create and audit readable body, heading and code typography at 200 percent zoom.

examples.title: Readable text metrics: concrete case
examples.situation: Article text uses a bounded measure and unitless line-height while containers expand for longer translated labels.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: Article text uses a bounded measure and unitless line-height while containers expand for longer translated labels.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Prioritize legibility and reflow with scalable text, adequate line height, and layouts that survive zoom and longer content.
examples.result: The result follows because Prioritize legibility and reflow with scalable text, adequate line height, and layouts that survive zoom and longer content.

examples.title: Readable text metrics: boundary case
examples.situation: Optimal values vary by script, language, content, and font; user overrides and 200 percent zoom must remain usable.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Optimal values vary by script, language, content, and font; user overrides and 200 percent zoom must remain usable.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Smaller text always fits more content and therefore improves usability.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Prioritize legibility and reflow with scalable text, adequate line height, and layouts that survive zoom and longer content.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Smaller text always fits more content and therefore improves usability.
misconceptions.correction: Prioritize legibility and reflow with scalable text, adequate line height, and layouts that survive zoom and longer content.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: Smaller text always fits more content and therefore improves usability.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Readable text metrics, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Readable text metrics before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.

recap.summary: You should now be able to create and audit readable body, heading and code typography at 200 percent zoom. Keep this boundary in view: Optimal values vary by script, language, content, and font; user overrides and 200 percent zoom must remain usable.

Apply checkpoint 'survive zoom' to a new artifact.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Color and contrast”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
